evidence: Contract governance checks are split across modules: `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates canonical action/startram catalog bindings while `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` independently re-validates the same upload action registry and payload-rule contract.

`goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go` defines cross-package governance policy text, while canonical contract IDs/specs are defined in catalog packages (`goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/startram/specs.go`), leaving policy, spec, and enforcement distributed across package boundaries.

`TestStartramContractSpecsMatchCanonicalCatalog` in `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` discovers ownership via string prefix filtering (`strings.HasPrefix`) against IDs built in `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/startram/specs.go`, coupling architecture checks to naming taxonomy instead of an explicit module boundary API.

Large callback-bag runtimes are used as generic dependency containers rather than cohesive domain abstractions (e.g., `startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` in startup orchestrator, `importerRuntime` with embedded sub-runtimes, and `workflowRuntime` embedding multiple orchestration op groups).

Multiple packages expose paired default-entry and runtime-injected entrypoints with minimal behavior difference (`StartBroadcastLoop` vs `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `runBroadcastTick` vs `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`, `DispatchUploadImport` vs `DispatchUploadImportWithCoordinator`, `StartBackupRoutines` vs `StartBackupRoutinesWithContext`).

A pass-through delegation layer (`DelegatingEventBroker`) mirrors all `EventBroker` methods without adding policy/translation, while callers still use concrete defaults (`events.DefaultEventRuntime()`) directly.

goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go states open-endpoint requires a complete endpoint/token payload, but goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go only tests presence/absence of OpenEndpointRequest and ResetRequest pointers, not field completeness semantics.

README.md quality gate command scopes contract conformance testing to `./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`; this misses runtime-side quality-gate coverage for non-upload action owners declared in goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go (e.g., c2c owned by system/wifi).

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go validates catalog/fixture coherence and payload-rule overlap, but no assigned test verifies end-to-end consumer behavior for C2C contract usage analogous to uploadsvc runtime validation coverage.

Fail-fast startup checks are time-bounded to 250ms in `waitForFailFastErrors` and return nil on timeout (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`), while `Bootstrap` performs a single `o.health(ctx)` call at startup end, so delayed worker failures can be missed entirely.

Wi-Fi nmcli parsers keep parse errors but only return one when no valid rows were parsed (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`), so malformed command output can degrade silently while API methods still return success (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`).

Some paths preserve typed publish-policy handling (e.g. importer transition publish uses `transition.HandleTransitionPublishError`), but neighboring paths still collapse error context to plain strings for state (`AddSystemTransitionError(err.Error())` in broadcast tick flow).

Upload transition type semantics are encoded as raw strings (`"status"`, `"patp"`, `"extracted"`, `"error"`) in policy and publish helpers (`goseg/importer/importer.go`) instead of a shared typed contract.

Workflow transition lifecycles also emit string literals (e.g., `ErrorEvent: func(_ error) string { return "error" }`) in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, duplicating event vocabulary outside a single authority.

The reviewed package set mixes strongly typed transition channels (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`) with stringly-typed event values in higher-level workflow/import flows, creating contract drift risk across boundaries.

findings.summary: Contract governance responsibility is split across catalog, conformance tests, and uploadsvc policy docs.
evidence: Catalog packages define canonical IDs/specs (`specs.go` files), but enforcement is duplicated in both `protocol/contracts/conformance` and `uploadsvc` tests.

`uploadsvc/doc.go` states canonical governance policy location, yet uploadsvc tests still encode cross-package registry invariants (`contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs`, contract metadata, payload rules).
This creates multiple architectural control points for one contract boundary, increasing blast radius for any contract model change.
findings.suggestion: Create a single governance verification surface under `protocol/contracts` (e.g., exported conformance helpers or shared fixture assertions) and have `uploadsvc` tests consume that API for boundary checks while keeping uploadsvc-local behavior tests only for parser/validator behavior.

findings.summary: Startram boundary validation relies on string-prefix scanning instead of explicit typed membership.
evidence: `TestStartramContractSpecsMatchCanonicalCatalog` builds a prefix from family/scope constants and filters `contracts.ContractCatalogEntries()` with `strings.HasPrefix`.
The test asserts module ownership by ID text shape (`startram.errors.*`) rather than by explicit catalog grouping API, so taxonomy renames alter boundary semantics across modules.
findings.suggestion: Expose explicit family-scoped catalog accessors (e.g., `contracts.StartramContractEntries()` or typed family tags) and update conformance tests to validate through that boundary, removing architectural dependence on ID string format.

findings.summary: Runtime structs have grown into broad callback bags that obscure real domain boundaries and reduce abstraction leverage.
evidence: `startupInitRuntime` and `StartupRuntime` embed multiple grouped runtime structs plus many function seams, then compose long step lists with policy wrappers, indicating orchestration and dependency wiring are coupled in one abstraction.

`importerRuntime` embeds storage/upload/provision groups and carries many callbacks (validation, filesystem, workflow, transition publishing), then aliases the same bag as init/workflow/publish runtime types.

`workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps` plus workflow-specific seams; this mixes unrelated concerns (container operations, DNS lookup, transition emission, upload orchestration) in one contract.
findings.suggestion: Split callback bags into narrower role-specific ports (e.g., `TransitionPublisher`, `ShipConfigStore`, `ContainerControl`, `StartupSubsystemRegistry`) and pass only the required role to each use case. Replace embedded mega-runtimes with constructor-wired services per workflow path.

findings.summary: Repeated default/WithRuntime wrapper chains add call depth without adding policy or translation.
evidence: Broadcast loop path layers through helper constructors and wrappers (`StartBroadcastLoop` -> `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime` -> `runBroadcastLoop` -> `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`), while many wrappers only perform nil checks or default resolution.
State lifecycle exposes near-duplicate runtime wrappers (`LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` forwarding to `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`, plus similar runtime/default split for reload).
Workflow and routine packages repeat this pattern (`DispatchUploadImport` -> `DispatchUploadImportWithCoordinator`; `StartBackupRoutines` forwarding to another package) with minimal abstraction gain.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrapper layers into one explicit public path per use case: keep either default-only entrypoints or injected-runtime entrypoints, not both across all operations. Where both are required, enforce a single boundary adapter that adds concrete behavior (policy, telemetry, retries), not just forwarding.

findings.summary: The event broker delegate abstraction duplicates the same contract with nearly pure pass-through methods.
evidence: `DelegatingEventBroker` implements each `EventBroker` method by nil-checking and forwarding to `delegate` without adding cross-cutting behavior.
Callers still capture concrete defaults (`events.DefaultEventRuntime()` in workflow and importer), so the delegation layer does not centralize policy or hide implementation changes.
This creates another indirection surface while concrete publish semantics are already defined by `EventRuntime` + `publishDropOnFull`.
findings.suggestion: Remove `DelegatingEventBroker` unless it enforces real policy (e.g., tracing/metrics/retry/backpressure translation). If policy is needed, make that policy explicit in a single decorator used by runtime constructors and stop direct default-runtime capture in downstream packages.

findings.summary: Mutable package-level default runtimes leak across boundaries and undermine explicit dependency abstractions.
evidence: Broadcast state and loop maintain mutable globals with setters/resets (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastLoopController`, `SetDefaultBroadcastLoopController`).
Events runtime also exposes mutable process-global state (`defaultEventRuntime`, `SetDefaultEventRuntime`, `ResetDefaultEventRuntime`).
This pattern allows hidden cross-module mutation and encourages callers to bypass explicit dependency injection, reducing interface honesty and making abstraction boundaries implicit.
findings.suggestion: Constrain mutable globals to bootstrap-only assembly and pass immutable runtime instances down from constructors. Deprecate public `SetDefault*` APIs in favor of context/service wiring so runtime ownership is explicit.

findings.summary: Action contract governance mixes upload and c2c ownership domains while process-level quality gates are upload-centric, creating a boundary integrity gap.
evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go defines NamespaceUpload and NamespaceC2C contracts together, with OwnerUploadService and OwnerSystemWiFi respectively.
README.md binds contract conformance gate to `./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`, which does not include the declared C2C runtime-owner module path.
goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go only asserts C2C fixture/catalog coherence, not owner-runtime behavior conformance.
findings.suggestion: Split governance into explicit per-owner contract families or add owner-scoped conformance suites and CI gates (e.g., include system/wifi owner runtime tests when c2c contracts exist). Keep contract catalog ownership and quality-gate scope mechanically aligned.

findings.summary: Contract conformance gate coverage does not include runtime modules for all declared action owners, so owner-specific regressions can pass green.
evidence: README.md backend contract gate command only runs `./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`.
goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go declares C2C action owner as OwnerSystemWiFi, indicating a non-upload runtime consumer.
goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go verifies metadata coherence for C2C but does not exercise C2C runtime owner behavior.
findings.suggestion: Expand the contract quality gate to include owner-runtime test targets for every action owner declared in the catalog, and add a failing guard test that detects unmapped owner modules in CI gate configuration.

findings.summary: Upload action tests do not validate the documented requirement that open-endpoint payload fields be complete, leaving a docs-vs-runtime contract blind spot.
evidence: goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go states open-endpoint requires a complete endpoint/token payload.
goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go checks missing OpenEndpointRequest pointer and forbidden payload mixing, but has no cases for empty Endpoint, TokenID, or TokenValue fields.
The helper commandForUploadBinding always generates non-empty endpoint/token values, so current positive-path tests cannot detect acceptance of incomplete payload fields.
findings.suggestion: Add table-driven negative tests for open-endpoint payload field completeness (empty endpoint, empty token id, empty token value, and combinations) and assert specific validation errors to align runtime behavior with the documented contract.

findings.summary: Fail-fast startup errors are only observed in a short window, then silently ignored.
evidence: `waitForFailFastErrors` returns nil when its 250ms timeout expires even if fail-fast workers are still running and may fail later.
`Bootstrap` invokes health once (`return o.health(ctx)`), so there is no ongoing reconciliation for delayed fail-fast worker errors during normal runtime.
Tests validate near-immediate fail-fast errors (e.g., 20ms), but do not enforce behavior for failures after the timeout window.
findings.suggestion: Make fail-fast monitoring persistent: store terminal worker errors in shared state and have `health` report them without a fixed startup-only timeout; alternatively keep a supervisor goroutine that propagates late fail-fast errors to a shared fatal channel consumed by startup/runtime orchestration.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi parsing tolerates malformed nmcli rows without surfacing partial-failure context.
evidence: `parseNmcliWifiDeviceRecords` and `parseNmcliWifiScanResults` collect parse errors but only return an error when no valid rows were parsed.
API calls like `ListWiFiSSIDs` treat parser success as fully valid output and return nil error, so malformed rows are silently dropped.
This yields mixed semantics: severe parser issues are strict when output is empty, but non-strict when output is partially parseable, making failures harder to diagnose.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed partial-parse error (or warning payload) that includes dropped-row count and first parse cause; propagate it through `ListWiFiSSIDs`/device resolution so callers can distinguish clean success from degraded parsing.

findings.summary: Transition event-type contracts are duplicated as string literals across workflow and importer paths.
evidence: Importer publish policy and constructors branch on literal type strings (`"status"`, `"patp"`, `"extracted"`, `"error"`).
Workflow transition lifecycle emits the same semantic state using a hardcoded literal (`"error"`) in event planning.
In parallel, event transport uses typed transition channels, so the type contract is not enforced uniformly end-to-end.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared typed transition-event vocabulary (constants or dedicated type with constructors) and replace ad-hoc string literals in importer/workflow publishers; keep compile-time checked conversions at transport boundaries.
